title: The Whitest Man I Know
content:
& R. Fenton Gower


آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s acruisinï؟½ in a pearler with a dirty nigger crew,
آ آ آ آ Abuyinï؟½ pearls and copra for a stingy Spanish Jew,
آ آ آ آ And his face is tannï؟½d like leather ï؟½neath a blazinï؟½ tropic Sun,
آ آ آ آ And heï؟½s workinï؟½ out a penance for the things he hasnï؟½t done.

آ آ آ آ Round the Solomons he runs, tradinï؟½ beads and castoff guns,
آ آ آ آ Buyinï؟½ pearls from grinninï؟½ niggers, loadin copra by the ton;
آ آ آ آ And heï؟½ll bargain and heï؟½ll smile, but heï؟½s thinkinï؟½ all the while
آ آ آ آ Of the penance that heï؟½s workinï؟½ out for sins he hasnï؟½t done.

آ آ آ آ Weï؟½d been round the Horn together, and Iï؟½d come to know his worth;
آ آ آ آ The greatest friend Iï؟½d ever had, the whitest man on earth.
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½d pullï؟½d me out of many a scrape, heï؟½d riskï؟½d his life for me,
آ آ آ آ And side by side, for many a year, weï؟½d roughï؟½d it on the sea;

آ آ آ آ But a woman came between us; she was beautiful as Venus,
آ آ آ آ And she set her cap at him until she hookï؟½d him unawares:
آ آ آ آ And I sailed off on my own
آ آ آ آ Leavinï؟½ him and her alone:
آ آ آ آ Signï؟½d aboard a tramp for ï؟½Frisco, leavinï؟½ them in Buï؟½nos Ayres.

آ آ آ آ When I met him in a twelvemonth he was goinï؟½ to the deuce,
آ آ آ آ For sheï؟½s blackenï؟½d all the good in him, sheï؟½d playï؟½d him fast and loose,
آ آ آ آ And sheï؟½d gone off with a Dago who was lettinï؟½ dollars fly,
آ آ آ آ And sheï؟½d left my mate to drink his precious soul away and die.

آ آ آ آ Well, I talkï؟½d and talkï؟½d him over, and we signï؟½d aboard ï؟½The Rover.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ It was just like good old times, until we shorï؟½d at Rio Bay;
آ آ آ آ Then the hand of Fate showï؟½d plain brought us face to face again
آ آ آ آ With the woman, and the Dago who had taken her away.

آ آ آ آ We were sittinï؟½in a cafe when the couple came along,
آ آ آ آ She simply smilï؟½d and passï؟½d us by, then vanishï؟½d in the throng.
آ آ آ آ My mate jumpï؟½d up to follow, but I wouldnï؟½t let him stir,
آ آ آ آ And later on a waiter brought a note that came from her:
آ آ آ آ She pretended she regretted
آ آ آ آ What sheï؟½d done, and that she fretted
آ آ آ آ For the wrong that she had done him, and she wanted to atone;
آ آ آ آ There was so much to explain,
آ آ آ آ Would he meet her once again
آ آ آ آ After midnight, in her garden she would watch for him, alone.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Course he went, but unbeknown to him I followï؟½d on behind.
آ آ آ آ I watchï؟½d, and saw the shadows of two figures on the blind
آ آ آ آ The woman and the Dago and I heard the Dago shout,
آ آ آ آ They quarrellï؟½d, and the woman screamï؟½d and then a shot rang out.
آ آ آ آ My mate dashï؟½d throï؟½ the curtain
آ آ آ آ And I followï؟½d, makinï؟½certain
آ آ آ آ That my little gun was ready case I had to make a stand:
آ آ آ آ There I saw the Dago dead,
آ آ آ آ With a bullet throï؟½ his head,
آ آ آ آ And the woman standinï؟½ near him with a shooter in her hand.

آ آ آ آ Before the Civil Guard came in my mate had snatchï؟½d her gun,
آ آ آ آ And he askï؟½d them to arrest him for the thing he hadnï؟½t done.
آ آ آ آ I tried madly to explain things, but they shook their heads at me,
آ آ آ آ And the woman let them take him, so that she might get off free.
آ آ آ آ In the court I sat and heard her
آ آ آ آ Tell them all heï؟½d done the murder,
آ آ آ آ And I prayï؟½d she might be stricken into some shape,
آ آ آ آ He was sentencï؟½d for his life
آ آ آ آ But out there corruptionï؟½s rife,
آ آ آ آ And I bribï؟½d and bribï؟½d, until at last I managï؟½d his escape.

آ آ آ آ Then I stowï؟½d him on a hooker sailinï؟½ far from womanï؟½s wiles,
آ آ آ آ And heï؟½s workinï؟½ his salvation out amongst the South Sea Isles;
آ آ آ آ And the womanï؟½s there at Rio, and sheï؟½s weavinï؟½ of her spell,
آ آ آ آ With a crowd of fools awaitinï؟½ her commands to burn in hell;
آ آ آ آ Whilst the whitest man I know
آ آ آ آ Runs a Christy minstrel show,
آ آ آ آ Buyinï؟½pearls from dirty niggers ï؟½neath a blazin tropic sun,
آ آ آ آ And heï؟½ll cussï؟½em, and heï؟½ll smile
آ آ آ آ But heï؟½s thinkinï؟½ all the while
آ آ آ آ Of the penance that heï؟½s workinï؟½out for things he hasnï؟½t done.
meaning:
This evocative piece by John Milton Hayes represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of John Milton Hayes's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
